coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted multiple-mode assay of ascorbic acid using cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes and carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Bo
Guo, Yang
Ma, Yongjun
Zhou, Min
Zhao, Youquan
Wang, Jingfeng
Fang, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: A multi-mode detection platform for ascorbic acid (AA) based on cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes (CoOOH) was designed in this study. CoOOH has enzyme-mimicking properties and can oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) into a blue colored oxidation product (TMBox). CoOOH nanoflakes are selectively reduced to Co2+ by AA, which results in the loss of oxidation of CoOOH and a gradual color evolution from blue to colorless and a UV–vis absorption decrease of TMBox. The AA can be detected in the range of 0.2–20 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.067 μM (UV–vis model). In addition, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method and employed as the signal output. When nanocomposites of CoOOH nanoflakes and CQDs were formed by hybridization, the fluorescence of CQDs was quenched by CoOOH nanoflakes via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and readily recovered by AA, representing an “on–off-on” response and a variation in color from black to light blue under a 365-nm UV lamp. The calibration curve was linear in the range of 0.1–30 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.031 μM (Fluorescence model). Two smartphone-based colorimetric devices were designed for analyzing the chromogenic solutions. The red, green, and blue values of the colored solutions were obtained directly using an Android application (Color Grab). The LOD of two smartphone colorimetry models are 0.16 μM and 0.19 μM, and linear range are 0.5–16 μM, 0.75–20 μM respectively. The multi-mode detection platform was successfully applied to the detection of AA in fruits with acceptable recoveries from 90.5% to 107%, a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 1.6% to 5.0%. Compared with the fluorescence and UV–vis modes, the smartphone-integrated colorimetric platform was found to be simple, portable, fast, and inexpensive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
CoOOH nanoflakes
TMB
Carbon quantum dots
Smartphone
RGB

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of AA via UV–vis and fluorescence analyses
Determination of AA in real samples

P. R. China 2021HK176, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42177439, 21767026 & 21862019) for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis-based and (B) fluorescence-based smartphone-assisted colorimetric boxes.

Colorimetric detection. (A) Photographs of the CoOOH-TMB system with different concentrations of AA. (B) Fluorescence images of the CoOOH-CQD system in the presence of various concentrations of AA. Changes of corresponding channel values in RGB space under different concentrations of ascorbic acid in COOOH-TMB system (C) and (D) in COOOH-CQDs system.
Schematic of the bimodal determination of ascorbic acid.
Schematic of the smartphone-based platform for ascorbic acid determination.
Comparison of the current method with reported methods.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted multiple-mode assay of ascorbic acid using cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes and carbon quantum dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: This author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multi-mode detection platform for ascorbic acid (AA) based on cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes (CoOOH) was designed in this study. CoOOH has enzyme-mimicking properties and can oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) into a blue colored oxidation product (TMBox). CoOOH nanoflakes are selectively reduced to Co2+ by AA, which results in the loss of oxidation of CoOOH and a gradual color evolution from blue to colorless and a UV–vis absorption decrease of TMBox. The AA can be detected in the range of 0.2–20 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.067 μM (UV–vis model). In addition, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with blue fluorescence were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method and employed as the signal output. When nanocomposites of CoOOH nanoflakes and CQDs were formed by hybridization, the fluorescence of CQDs was quenched by CoOOH nanoflakes via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and readily recovered by AA, representing an “on–off-on” response and a variation in color from black to light blue under a 365-nm UV lamp. The calibration curve was linear in the range of 0.1–30 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.031 μM (Fluorescence model). Two smartphone-based colorimetric devices were designed for analyzing the chromogenic solutions. The red, green, and blue values of the colored solutions were obtained directly using an Android application (Color Grab). The LOD of two smartphone colorimetry models are 0.16 μM and 0.19 μM, and linear range are 0.5–16 μM, 0.75–20 μM respectively. The multi-mode detection platform was successfully applied to the detection of AA in fruits with acceptable recoveries from 90.5% to 107%, a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 1.6% to 5.0%. Compared with the fluorescence and UV–vis modes, the smartphone-integrated colorimetric platform was found to be simple, portable, fast, and inexpensive.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is a micronutrient that plays an important role in various physiological processes [1]. AA is a vital cofactor in several neurotransmitter-related enzyme reactions and can scavenge free radicals to reduce oxidative stress [2]. The main sources of ascorbic acid are fresh vegetables, fruits, beverages, and AA-containing drugs. However, excessive and long-term intake of AA can result in various related diseases, such as abdominal pain, rashes, and even blood clots and cancer [3]. Therefore, accurate analysis of AA is critical in food quality control and disease diagnosis. Traditional methods for AA analysis include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], fluorescence [5], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6], electrochemiluminescence [7], and capillary electrophoresis [8]. Although these techniques possess extremely effective capacities with high sensitivities and selectivities in certain harsh scenarios, most of them require time-consuming pre-treatment steps, expensive equipment, and skilled training. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a more rapid and simpler determination method for AA.

Nanomaterials have been extensively applied for the determination of AA [9-11]. Two-dimensional nanomaterials such as manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2) and cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes (CoOOH) have drawn considerable attention because of their excellent properties such as large surface area, wide UV-absorption band, and oxidase-like activity [12]. MnO2 nanosheets and CoOOH nanoflakes have been employed as excellent alternatives to natural enzymes for the detection of antioxidants in red wine [13], glutathione [14] and glucosidase inhibitors in whole HeLa cells [15]. Moreover, these two-dimensional nanomaterials have been utilized for the detection of alkaline phosphatase [16] in human serum samples owing to their wide absorption band.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have a typical zero-dimensional structure and are considered a significant and novel type of fluorescent nanomaterial with excellent optical properties. Compared to conventional organic fluorescent dyes, CQDs have promising characteristics such as decent water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and straightforward operation [17,18]. They have been extensively applied in bioimaging [19], nanoprobe [20], sensors [21] and other fields [22]. The combination of CoOOH nanoflakes and CQDs is known to facilitate convenient determination of AA [23-25]. Although these materials have been adopted in conjunction with various methods to detect AA, a single-signal output might limit the accuracy of the analysis. Therefore, a multi-signal output platform [26-28] has been introduced for AA detection.

Smartphone-assisted platforms enable the possibility of performing tedious and intricate experimental procedures in a simple manner, and can act as communication media, advanced transducers [29], and mobile biosensors [30,31]. Smartphones have been successfully employed for the analysis of chemicals and biochemicals [32-34], pesticide residues [35], environmental pollution [36] and water turbidity sensing [37].

In this study, a multiple-mode AA assay based on CoOOH nanoflakes and carbon quantum dots was developed. The CoOOH nanoflakes efficiently oxidized TMB into an oxidation product, TMBox, without additional H2O2, owing to their oxidase-like activity. The UV absorption intensity expectedly changed because of the loss of oxidizability of CoOOH nanoflakes in the presence of AA. Moreover, CoOOH nanoflakes quenched the fluorescence of CQDs based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [23]. The fluorescence of the CQDs was recovered when the CoOOH nanoflakes decomposed and collapsed upon the introduction of AA. The presence of AA led to opposite color changes in the CoOOH-TMB and CoOOH-CQD sensors. The intense color changes of blue colored TMBox and blue fluorescent CQDs were feasibly captured using a smartphone with the Color Grab application [38]. The smartphone was used as a signal reader to obtain raw color values representing red (R), green (G), and blue (B) for the quantitative analysis. Credible detection of AA in AA tablets and fresh orange was further verified.

TMB was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, ascorbic acid, and uric acid were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Proline, tyrosine, tryptophan, serine, cysteine, methionine, histidine, cysteine, and lysine were purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium acetate, acetic acid, and other metal salts were supplied by Aladdin. All reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained using the Millipore water purification system. Various types of homemade colorimetric boxes were used in this study. A description of the instruments used in the experiments has been provided in the Electronic Supporting Material (ESM).

A previously reported method was used with minor modifications to prepare the CoOOH nanoflakes [26]. Briefly, a mixture of 500 µL of NaClO (0.9 M) and 400 µL of NaOH (1 M) was added to 1 mL of CoCl2 (10 mM). The mixture was subsequently centrifuged at 5000 rpm after sonication for 10 min, resulting in CoOOH nanoflakes being obtained as the precipitate. Finally, the precipitate was dissolved in 1 mL of ultra-pure water and sonicated for 5 min.

CQDs were synthesized using a previously reported one-step hydrothermal method [39]. An L-proline solution (1.15 g/20 mL) was transferred into a 50-mL sealed polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brownish yellow solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min and filtered using Supor filters (0.45 µm). The supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (MW = 3.5 kDa) and refrigerated at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.
The CoOOH nanoflake solution (135 μL) was mixed with 200 μL of CQDs in a NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7) and sonicated for 5 min for subsequent analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA via UV–vis and fluorescence analyses
ce:section.ce:para: CoOOH solution (180 μL) was first incubated with different concentrations of AA in 1800 μL of the NaAc-HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH = 4.5). Subsequently, 200 μL of TMB (1 mM) was injected into the preceding solution and maintained at room temperature for 5 min. Finally, the absorbance at 652 nm was measured using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer.

Various concentrations of AA were added to the CoOOH-CQD nanocomposite solution, and the mixtures were incubated for 6 min to reach equilibrium. Fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded in the 350–550 nm range under excitation at 350 nm upon the addition of AA. The relationship between (A 0 − A)/(F − F 0) and the AA concentration was examined. Here, A and A 0 represent the UV–vis absorption intensity in the presence and absence of AA, respectively. F and F 0 refer to the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of AA, respectively.

After introducing AA into the system, the colors of the CoOOH-TMB and CoOOH-CQD mixed solution were captured using the Color Grab application with a smartphone in two different homemade black boxes (Fig. 1 ). These portable devices were constructed using black plastic materials, which prevented the entry and interference of ambient light. The two boxes were each used for visible colorimetry and fluorescent colorimetry, respectively.

Fig. S4 indicates that AA and TMB do not show any significant UV–vis absorption between 330 and 700 nm. A clear absorption peak at 652 nm is observed after the incubation of TMB with CoOOH nanoflakes at room temperature, indicating the production of the blue colored monoelectron oxidation product, TMBox. This is primarily because of the two ammonia groups in TMB that are readily oxidized via donation of electrons. However, the CoOOH nanoflakes are reduced to Co2+ upon the addition of AA. The UV–vis absorption intensity of TMBox gradually decreases as the blue color fades. The subsequent quantitative analysis was carried out in accordance with the UV–vis absorbance changes of TMBox at 652 nm.

The catalytic kinetics of CoOOH-TMB were also investigated. The apparent kinetic parameter can be determined using the Lineweaver–Burk curve derived from the double-reciprocal equation of the Michaelis–Menten plot (Fig. S5). The Lineweaver–Burk equation is expressed as follows:
(1) 1/V = K m/[S] + 1/V max

where [S] represents the substrate concentration, and V max represents the maximum enzymatic reaction rate. K m represents the substrate concentration corresponding to half of the maximum reaction speed, and is a characteristic constant of enzymes; it is an important parameter in enzymology and describes various types of non-isomerase kinetics. K m can be approximated to reflect the affinity of the enzyme to the substrate. K m was evaluated to be 0.0286 in this study, which is lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [40]. The mimetic enzyme activity of the material is usually compared with that of peroxidase. The lower the value of K m, the greater the affinity of the enzyme to the substrate [41]. This may be attributed to the large specific surface area and numerous active sites of hexagonal hydroxyl [42,43] in the synthesized cobalt oxide nanoflakes.

The fluorescence spectra show that the CQDs exhibit maximum fluorescence at 428 nm under excitation of 350 nm, with the excitation-dependent features showing blue fluorescence (Fig. S6). The stability of the CQDs was systematically investigated using fluorescence spectra in an aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S7, the blue CQDs exhibit decent photostability and can maintain a constant color for 7 h. The UV–vis absorption band of CoOOH overlaps considerably with the fluorescence emission band of the CQDs (Fig. S8A), which satisfies the conditions of the FRET mechanism [23]. The sensing system of CoOOH-CQDs for AA detection was tested by adsorbing CQDs onto the surface of the CoOOH nanoflakes. In this scenario, the 428 nm fluorescence response of the CQDs significantly decays, and the bright blue fluorescence is quenched. In the presence of AA, the specific reducibility of AA facilitates the reduction of CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+ and destroys the relationship between CoOOH (acceptor) and CQDs (donor), thereby inhibiting energy transfer. The fluorescence is effectively recovered, resulting in a color change from black to light blue (Fig. S8B). Scheme 1 shows the mechanism of AA detection in this study.

The colorimetric signals from UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy were captured using a smartphone in homemade signal readout devices. The RGB values of the solution were picked by Color Grab, which is a free Android application, without the need for any additional image processing. The smartphone-based platform used for AA detection in this study is outlined in Scheme 2 .

The concentration of CoOOH, pH of the buffer, and incubation time in both sensing systems were optimized to obtain conditions appropriate for AA detection. The optimal conditions were assessed by observing the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence intensities. As shown in Fig. S9A, the catalytic oxidation ability of CoOOH nanoflakes gradually increases with an increase in the concentration from 0.1 mM to 0.7 mM. The absorption at 652 nm does not change drastically when the concentration of CoOOH is higher than 0.7 mM. Therefore, the optimum concentration of CoOOH was determined to be 0.7 mM. The quenching effect of the CoOOH nanoflakes was reconsidered for the fluorescence mode. Various concentrations of CoOOH nanoflakes were incubated with 200 μL of CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs is observed to clearly decrease with increasing amounts of CoOOH. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is up to 90 % in the 0.1–0.5 mM range (Fig. S9B). However, the fluorescence intensity slightly decreases in the 0.6–0.8 mM range. Notably, excessive amounts of CoOOH nanoflakes could not be disintegrated by AA, resulting in a decrease in sensitivity. Therefore, 0.5 mM was selected as the optimal concentration of CoOOH in this study.

Fig. S10A indicates that the values of A 0 − A increase with increasing pH from 2 to 4, and plateau in the pH range of 4–5; the values drop when the pH is above 5. Therefore, the optimal catalytic oxidation activity presumably occurs at a pH below 4.5, with the highest oxidizing capacity. In the CoOOH-CQD system, the values of F − F 0 reach a maximum at pH = 7 and decrease when the pH value is greater than 8, resulting in a poor detection efficiency. Therefore, a value of approximately 7.0 was selected as the suitable pH (Fig. S10B). TMB was subsequently incubated with the CoOOH and AA mixtures by varying the reaction time from 0 to 10 min. The values of A 0 − A are observed to gradually increase with increasing reaction times (Fig. S10C). The reaction was noted to take 5 min to reach equilibrium and almost maintains a stable trend thereafter, indicating the rapid response and decent stability of the CoOOH-TMB-based system for AA detection. In the fluorescence mode, the F − F 0 values are noted to increase until an equilibrium is reached after 6 min (Fig. S10D). Both CoOOH-TMB and CoOOH-CD systems clearly exhibit rapid responses, which indicate their tremendous potential for rapid detection.

The AA assay was successfully implemented and offered sensitive detection under the optimized conditions. The A 0 − A values were evaluated under different AA concentrations in the CoOOH-TMB system (Fig. S11A). The UV–vis absorption at 652 nm is observed to decrease as the AA concentration increases. A0 − A exhibits a linear relationship with the concentration of AA in the range of 0.2–20 μM (R2 = 0.9964, ΔA = 0.3232C + 0.1966) with a lower LOD of 0.067 μM. The LOD was calculated using the 3σ/slope rule, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank sample solution.

The fluorescence intensity at 428 nm was employed as the signal readout. As shown in Fig. S11B, the emission at 428 nm is gradually restored with increasing amounts of AA. F − F0 displays decent linearity with the concentration of AA in the range of 0.1–30 μM (R2 = 0.9986, ΔF = 2.99 × 105C + 4.54 × 105). The fluorescence mode provides an acceptable sensitivity, accompanied by a lower LOD of 0.031 μM. These results demonstrate the feasibility of the developed sensing system for AA detection. The dual-signal tactics demonstrate the advantages of the developed AA assay with original “signal off” and “signal on” readouts.

During the smartphone-based colorimetric detection, the color of TMBox is observed to visibly change from blue to colorless (Fig. 2 A) with increasing amounts of AA. The values of R, G and B channels gradually increase with the amount of AA added (Fig. 2C). Accurate quantitative analysis was accomplished by establishing the relationship between the concentration of AA and R, G, B values. The results are listed for comparison in Table S1. Among the various quantitative models, the single-channel R values are noted to exhibit an optimal linear relationship with AA concentration from 0.5 μM to 16 μM (R2 = 0.9926). The linear equation was obtained as IR = 9.155C + 58.12, and the LOD was calculated to be 0.16 μM.

The fluorescent colors under 365-nm UV light transform from dark to light blue (Fig. 2B) with increasing AA concentrations and the intensity of R, G, B also changes (Fig. 2D). Different mathematical models based on R, G, B values were constructed to explore the relationship between the color value and AA concentration (Table S2). The data show that the single-channel B values increase continuously with increasing AA concentrations. A decent linear relationship was observed between the B values and AA concentration from 0.75 μM to 20 μM. The linear equation was obtained as IB = 8.691C + 83.86, and the LOD was evaluated to be 0.19 μM based on 3σ/slope. The constructed method was compared with some recently reported as shown in Table 1 . These results demonstrate that a simple and precise quantitative analysis can be performed in conjunction with smartphone colorimetry, which indicates current multi-mode detection platform can be considered as an outstanding sensor for detecting AA.

Different types of amino acids (glutathione, cysteine, lysine, proline, serine, tryptophan, tyrosine, and histidine), various common metal ions (Ni2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe3+, and K+), and other interfering chemicals (citric acid, dopamine, and uric acid) were used to study interference in the fluorescence and UV–vis modes. The concentrations of cysteine, glutathione, uric acid, and dopamine were 10 times that of AA. Those of all other interferers were 50 times the concentration of AA. When such diverse species are added separately, the turn-off effect of UV–vis (Fig.S12 A) and the turn-on effect of fluorescence (Fig. S12B) do not appear, suggesting that the interfering chemicals do not hinder the determination of AA. The dual mode exhibits fine selectivity toward AA against other chemicals because of the specific reduction of AA. The corresponding color images of TMBox under white LED light (Fig. S12C) and fluorescence images under a 365-nm UV lamp were recorded (Fig. S12D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence, UV–vis spectroscopy, and smartphone-based colorimetry were applied for AA detection in fresh orange and AA tablet to confirm the validity and applicability. The accuracy was examined with recovery studies. Different concentrations of AA were spiked into the samples for the recovery experiments. The relative recoveries are summarized in Table 2 , with the confidence interval for the mean of three parallel experiments (α = 0.05). Acceptable recoveries varying from 90.5% to 107% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 1.6% to 5.0%. These results are satisfactory and indicate that the AA can be accurately analyzed in these matrices when present within the detection limits.

Fluorescence detection mode was employed for AA detection in different samples including VC tablets, beverages, vegetables and fruits. The data were listed in Table 3 . The results were compared with those of 2, 6-dichloroindophenol (DCIP) titrimetric method [46] designated by the Association of Official Analytical Chemists (AOAC) [47]. The t values calculated from the paired t test were less than t (95%, 3) = 3.18. These data revealed there was no statistically significant difference between DCIP titration method and proposed method. The results suggest that the developed method is capable of quantitative determination of AA in real samples with high accuracy, selectivity and sensitivity. The smartphone-based colorimetric determination step is much simpler, faster, and cost-effective.

A novel cobalt-oxyhydroxide-nanoflake sensing system was successfully developed for the multi-mode detection of ascorbic acid. CoOOH-TMB and CoOOH-CQD were employed as signal indicators for accurate quantitative analysis owing to their wide detection range, low detection limit, high sensitivity, and decent selectivity. The intense color changes of the sensing system were captured using a smartphone-integrated platform for cost-effective quantitative colorimetric analysis. The compact, homemade, and smartphone-based readout devices can assist in visual food-safety monitoring and environmental pollution detection in the chemical and biological domains in a convenient and inexpensive manner.

Bo Peng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yang Guo: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Yongjun Ma: Data curation, Validation. Min Zhou: Software. Youquan Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Jingfeng Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yanjun Fang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603700), the special fund from key topics (N0: AWS18J004 & N0: BWS17J025), the Research project of General Administration of Customs. P. R. China 2021HK176, and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42177439, 21767026 & 21862019) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107185.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Am. Coll. Nutr.

J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.
